# UC-23.2.1 · Sales Pipeline Velocity and Forecast Accuracy

> **Criticality:** Critical &middot; **Difficulty:** Intermediate &middot; **Pillar:** Platform &middot; **Type:** Business &middot; **Status:** Verified

*We look at the Salesforce list of open deals, how much each is worth, and which forecast bucket it sits in, then compare that picture to what already closed. When the open pile is too thin for the revenue we still need, we say so in plain language before the quarter surprises the CEO.*

---

## Description

This detection runs over Salesforce Opportunity records—whether from **Bulk API 2.0** CSV into `salesforce:opportunity` or from a warehouse via **DB Connect**—and rolls up `StageName`, `Amount`, `Probability`, and `ForecastCategoryName` to expose pipeline coverage, weighted open pipeline, win rate, and average age of open opportunities. The SPL combines `IsWon`, `IsClosed`, and stage labels so Closed Won and Closed Lost land in the right buckets, then compares aggregate open `Amount` to closed-won `Amount` to flag quarters where pipeline coverage (open pipe ÷ won revenue) slips under a 3× guardrail. The result is a board-ready view tied to the same sObject field names a Salesforce admin checks in a report, not a generic CRM abstraction.

## Value

CFO, FP&A, and the VP of Sales use forward-looking pipeline coverage and forecast-category mix when they brief the board, set hiring and quota capacity, and decide capital allocation. When coverage falls weeks before a quarter close, teams get a quantified red flag before revenue misses show up in the ERP—protecting enterprise valuation narratives and rep accountability. Pairing the metric with week-over-week `weighted_value` and win-rate movement turns ad-hoc pipeline calls into a rhythm aligned with the `ForecastCategoryName` slices sales operations already uses in Salesforce (Pipeline, Best Case, Commit, Closed, Omitted).

## Implementation

Provision a **Connected App** with JWT, run **Bulk API 2.0** `queryAll` on `Opportunity` with a `LastModifiedDate` watermark, and ship one row per record to HEC as `salesforce:opportunity`. Optionally install `Splunk_TA_salesforce` (4146) for EventLogFile sourcetypes, and use **DB Connect** to the warehouse if the direct API is not viable. In Step 2, save the search as a Dashboard Studio panel with quarter tokens; route sub-3× coverage signals to FP&A and reserve executive paging to the Step 4 window.

## Detailed Implementation

### Prerequisites
- Heavy Forwarder or a mid-tier data-collection node with outbound HTTPS to `login.salesforce.com` (or your **My Domain** login host) and to the Splunk HEC endpoint; version aligned with `splunkVersions` above.
- A Salesforce org with API access: **Enterprise**, **Unlimited**, **Performance**, or **Developer** editions that include Bulk API 2.0 (Developer is fine for a pilot). The integration profile must have **API Enabled** and read access to the **Opportunity** object, including **View All** (or object-level read) and field-level read on **Amount**; hidden **Amount** from field-level security shows up as `null` in the REST payload, which silently zeroes your dollar rollups until fixed.
- Python 3.9+ (Ship with the app or the OS) and libraries for `jwt`, `cryptography` (load the Connected App’s private key), `requests`, and CSV streaming.
- Splunk Add-on for Salesforce (`Splunk_TA_salesforce`, Splunkbase 4146) is optional: install it on the same Heavy Forwarder and Search Heads if you want **EventLogFile** sourcetypes for context. It is **not** a substitute for pulling **Opportunity** rows — that add-on’s shipped inputs are built around the Event Monitoring product and **not** the full custom object stream.
- Splunk DB Connect (Splunkbase 2686) for Path B, with a database identity, connection, and one or more `db_input` stanzas.
- A `business` index (or retarget every `index=` token in the SPL and your role’s `srchIndexesAllowed` together).

**Step 1 — Configure data collection**
**Path A (primary):** Create a **Connected App** in **Setup → App Manager → New Connected App**. Enable OAuth, select **Use digital signatures**, upload the X.509 public half of a key you hold privately, and grant scopes `api`, `refresh_token`, and `offline_access` per Salesforce Help. Build a per-org **Connected App** consumer using the **JWT bearer** grant: `POST https://login.salesforce.com/services/oauth2/token` with `grant_type=urn:ietf:params:oauth:grant-type:jwt-bearer` and a signed `assertion` that includes the `aud`, `iss` (client id), `sub` (integration user’s username), and `exp` claims. A successful response returns an `access_token` and an `instance_url`.

Run **Bulk API 2.0** query jobs against that host: `POST {instance_url}/services/data/v59.0/jobs/query` with JSON such as `{"operation":"queryAll","query":"SELECT Id, StageName, Amount, CloseDate, CreatedDate, Probability, ForecastCategoryName, OwnerId, IsWon, IsClosed, RecordTypeId, Type, LeadSource, LastModifiedDate FROM Opportunity WHERE LastModifiedDate > 2024-01-01T00:00:00Z"}` — swap in your persisted **high-water mark** instead of a fixed literal in production. Poll `GET {instance_url}/services/data/v59.0/jobs/query/{id}` until `state` is **JobComplete** (or handle **Failed** and **InvalidJob** by inspecting `errorMessage`). Pull results from `.../jobs/query/{id}/results` as CSV, parse, and for each data row emit a HEC event with the raw fields plus `_time` set from `LastModifiedDate` (or `strptime` that column into epoch seconds) so the Splunk timeline matches Salesforce. Use `sourcetype=salesforce:opportunity` and `index=business` — this HEC name is a **per-deployment** convention, not a Splunkbase-builtin sourcetype list entry.

On indexers (or a heavy forwarder with parsing, depending on your topology), define the sourcetype so the fields become searchable. Example `props.conf`:

```
[salesforce:opportunity]
LINE_BREAKER = ([\r\n]+)
TRUNCATE = 1000000
TIME_FORMAT = %Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%S.%3N%z
MAX_TIMESTAMP_LOOKAHEAD = 32
```

Add `FIELDALIAS` or `EXTRACT-` stanzas if you serialize JSON and want **StageName** at the top level. Schedule the poller with headroom: a large `LIMIT`-less query can keep a job in **JobInProgress** for many minutes; keep incremental filters on **LastModifiedDate**, bound each run to 15–30 minutes of **Bulk API 2.0** polling, and back off on `REQUEST_LIMIT_EXCEEDED` or `DAILY_API_REQUESTS_EXCEEDED` responses. Enterprise orgs often have a 100,000-call 24h rolling window, while Unlimited is far higher; **Bulk API 2.0** batches tens of thousands of result rows per job without spending one call per record line.

Name the Python modular input deterministically, e.g. `salesforce_opp_bapi_v1`, and ship it in an app that contains `local/inputs.conf`:

```
[script://$SPLUNK_HOME/etc/apps/TA_custom_salesforce_opp/bin/poll_opportunities_to_hec.py]
interval = 1800
disabled = 0
sourcetype = salesforce:opportunity
index = business
```

(Use `interval` in seconds and tune after measuring job completion time.)

**Step 1b — Path B (warehouse fallback):** When direct API is politically impossible, use **DB Connect** to `SELECT` the replicated `opportunity` table your ELT job maintains. Example stanza in `db_inputs.conf` style (`db_inputs.conf` is merged by DB Connect; exact stanza name depends on the UI you used):

```
[db://snowflake_warehouse/opportunity_upsert]
connection = my_sf_replica
cron_schedule = 0 */4 * * *
sourcetype = dbx:opportunity
index = business
query = SELECT id AS opportunity_id, stage_name, amount, close_date, created_date, probability, forecast_category, owner_id, is_won, is_closed, last_modified_date FROM crm.sfdc_opportunity WHERE last_modified_date > ?
```

Map database columns to the SPL `coalesce()` aliases in the `spl` field. Expect 4–24h freshness versus near-real-time **Path A**, but far fewer `LIMIT` surprises against Salesforce itself.

**Step 2 — Create the search and alert**
Paste the same logic as the top-level `spl` field, then **Save As** → Report or **Alert**.

```spl
index=business (sourcetype="salesforce:opportunity" OR sourcetype="dbx:opportunity" OR sourcetype="dbx:crm_opportunities") earliest=-90d@d
| eval Id=coalesce(Id, id, opportunity_id, OpportunityId)
| eval StageName=coalesce(StageName, stage_name)
| eval Amount=tonumber(coalesce(Amount, amount, "0"))
| eval ForecastCategoryName=coalesce(ForecastCategoryName, forecast_category, "Unspecified")
| eval Probability=tonumber(coalesce(Probability, probability, "0"))
| eval CreatedDateStr=coalesce(CreatedDate, created_date)
| eval cd_date=if(isnotnull(CreatedDateStr) AND match(CreatedDateStr,"^\\d{4}-\\d{2}-\\d{2}"), strptime(replace(mvindex(split(CreatedDateStr,"T"),0),"Z",""),"%Y-%m-%d"), null())
| eval IsWon_f=if(like(StageName,"%Closed Won%") OR in(lower(tostring(coalesce(IsWon,is_won,""))),"1","true"),1,0)
| eval IsClosed_f=if(like(StageName,"%Closed Won%") OR like(StageName,"%Closed Lost%") OR in(lower(tostring(coalesce(IsClosed,is_closed,""))),"1","true"),1,0)
| eval open_opp=if(IsClosed_f=0,1,0)
| eval days_in_pipeline=if(isnotnull(cd_date), round((now()-cd_date)/86400,0), null())
| eval pipeline_amt=if(open_opp=1, Amount, 0)
| eval weighted_value=if(open_opp=1, Amount*Probability/100, null())
| eval won_amt=if(IsWon_f=1, Amount, 0)
| eval lost_amt=if(IsClosed_f=1 AND IsWon_f=0, Amount, 0)
| bin _time span=1d
| stats sum(pipeline_amt) as total_pipeline sum(weighted_value) as weighted_pipeline sum(won_amt) as won_revenue sum(lost_amt) as lost_revenue avg(eval(if(open_opp=1, days_in_pipeline, null()))) as avg_days_in_pipeline dc(Id) as opportunity_count sum(IsWon_f) as won_deals sum(eval(if(IsClosed_f=1 AND IsWon_f=0,1,0))) as lost_deals by ForecastCategoryName, _time
| eventstats sum(total_pipeline) as org_open_pipe sum(won_revenue) as org_won_revenue
| eval win_rate=if((won_deals+lost_deals)>0, round(100*won_deals/(won_deals+lost_deals),1), null())
| eval pipeline_coverage=if(org_won_revenue>0, round(org_open_pipe/nullif(org_won_revenue,0),2), null())
| eval coverage_flag=if(isnotnull(pipeline_coverage) AND pipeline_coverage<3, "below_3x", "ok")
| table _time, ForecastCategoryName, total_pipeline, weighted_pipeline, won_revenue, win_rate, avg_days_in_pipeline, pipeline_coverage, coverage_flag, opportunity_count
| sort _time, ForecastCategoryName
```

The opening **index/sourcetype** tuple intentionally unions the HEC path and a representative `dbx:*` name so the same report works during migrations. The **eval** stanzas normalize **StageName**-based and boolean **IsWon**/**IsClosed** signals so won vs open dollars stay aligned with the Salesforce UI. The **bin**+**stats** pass applies `bin _time span=1d` for a daily time series while preserving **ForecastCategoryName** (Pipeline, Best Case, Commit, Closed, Omitted) as a facet. The **eventstats** pass calculates org-wide `pipeline_coverage` as open **Amount** divided by closed-won **Amount**; treat **below_3x** on `coverage_flag` as the yellow threshold for FP&A, and chain a second alert (same SPL with a five-day time filter before quarter end) to page CFO and VP of Sales if coverage stays under 2.0. **win_rate** is there to catch sudden quality decay even when dollars still look large.

**Step 3 — Validate**
In Salesforce, open **Reports** → **All Reports** and run a standard **Opportunity Pipeline** report; also open any **Forecast Snapshot** (or **Forecasting** app) your org uses. Pick five `Id` values from the Splunk result table and look them up with the global **Opportunity** ID search — **Amount**, **StageName**, **ForecastCategoryName**, and **LastModifiedDate** should match within replication lag. In Splunk, run `| timechart count by date_mday span=1d` on `LastModifiedDate` to confirm there are no multi-day empty gaps in ingestion. For dollars, compare the Splunk `sum(Amount)` for closed-won rows over the same fiscal period to the report’s “Closed Won” total; variance usually traces to **FLS** on **Amount** for the integration user, duplicate ingests from a manual CSV replay, or sandbox rows indexed by mistake. Document any acceptable variance (for example, test sandboxes) in the runbook so audits stay clean.

**Step 4 — Operationalize**
Publish a **Dashboard Studio** board with the visualization mix from the `visualization` field: funnel by **StageName**, single value on **weighted_pipeline** with a quarter token, trellis coverage gauges, line overlays for the current and prior fiscal quarter, and a rep table keyed by **OwnerId**. Default the time picker to this quarter and overlay last quarter for narrative context. **Alert routing:** `coverage_flag=below_3x` and `pipeline_coverage` between 2.0 and 3.0 go to the FP&A shared mailbox for analysis **without** a page. Five working days or fewer before a fiscal quarter end, the same condition pages both **CFO** and **VP of Sales** if `pipeline_coverage<2.0` — the leadership pair stay named co-owners. Also schedule a week-over-week saved search on **win_rate**; if the quarter-over-quarter delta exceeds **15** percentage points, create a **calendar** hold titled “forecast recalibration” and invite RevOps, FP&A, and sales leadership (owner: **FP&A** on-call as ticket recipient). This cadence is intentionally heavier than a generic “monitor the dashboard” note because the metric feeds board and lending conversations.

**Step 5 — Troubleshooting**
- `invalid_client` or `error=invalid_request` on the JWT `POST` to `/services/oauth2/token` most often means the X.509 cert paired with the **Connected App** expired or the issuer/subject do not match the integration user. Rotate keys at least annually, keep a 30-day pre-expiry calendar task, and re-upload the new public cert before removing the old private key from the poller host.
- **Bulk API 2.0** jobs that remain **JobInProgress** longer than 30 minutes usually carry too many rows in one SOQL `WHERE` block or a hot, non-selective filter. Split the work by time slices on **LastModifiedDate** or add a `LIMIT` in test only; in production, prefer narrower windows. Watch for `LIMIT_EXCEEDED` in JSON error bodies on synchronous REST if someone mixes patterns.
- Responses with `DAILY_API_REQUESTS_EXCEEDED` or `REQUEST_LIMIT_EXCEEDED` call for fewer scheduled runs, lighter synchronous REST, and a strict `LastModifiedDate >` incremental pattern so each pass touches only the delta. When possible, keep heavy pulls on **Bulk API 2.0** instead of the synchronous **query** endpoint.
- **Amount** present but always zero in Splunk while the UI shows a balance means **field-level security** for the running user: **Setup → Object Manager → Opportunity → Fields → Amount → Set Field-Level Security** and grant read to the integration profile. Until then, the REST and CSV exports carry `null` and the SPL will understate revenue — there is no error line to grep.
- If **high-water mark** based on `LastModifiedDate` stops advancing, compare Splunk’s `_indextime` to Salesforce event times; more than a five-minute skew between NTP-disciplined hosts can strand rows near the cut. Fix NTP, or subtract 60–120 seconds in the poller as a **safety buffer** on the `WHERE` clause.
- A **sandbox refresh** typically deletes or re-creates the integration user and scatters `LastModifiedDate` on every **Opportunity**; treat the next poll as a **baseline rebuild** (reset checkpoint), rebind the **Connected App** OAuth consumer, and reapply profile permissions. Keep a one-page “sandbox-drift” runbook in Confluence/Notion that names the person who re-runs the **Bulk API 2.0** backfill.
- `INVALID_SESSION_ID` in downstream REST calls is an expired `access_token`; the Python worker should always refresh the token on **401** using the same JWT or refresh **OAuth** pattern you configured, not reuse a day-old string.
- Velocity math that double-counts long-cycle opps is almost always a **Closed Lost** → reopened → **Closed Won** sequence without **OpportunityFieldHistory**. Add a second ingest of stage history, or in Splunk join `Id` to a `lookup` maintained from a **Data Loader** export of that object, and compute transitions off history rows instead of net-new snapshots only.


## SPL

```spl
index=business (sourcetype="salesforce:opportunity" OR sourcetype="dbx:opportunity" OR sourcetype="dbx:crm_opportunities") earliest=-90d@d
| eval Id=coalesce(Id, id, opportunity_id, OpportunityId)
| eval StageName=coalesce(StageName, stage_name)
| eval Amount=tonumber(coalesce(Amount, amount, "0"))
| eval ForecastCategoryName=coalesce(ForecastCategoryName, forecast_category, "Unspecified")
| eval Probability=tonumber(coalesce(Probability, probability, "0"))
| eval CreatedDateStr=coalesce(CreatedDate, created_date)
| eval cd_date=if(isnotnull(CreatedDateStr) AND match(CreatedDateStr,"^\\d{4}-\\d{2}-\\d{2}"), strptime(replace(mvindex(split(CreatedDateStr,"T"),0),"Z",""),"%Y-%m-%d"), null())
| eval IsWon_f=if(like(StageName,"%Closed Won%") OR in(lower(tostring(coalesce(IsWon,is_won,""))),"1","true"),1,0)
| eval IsClosed_f=if(like(StageName,"%Closed Won%") OR like(StageName,"%Closed Lost%") OR in(lower(tostring(coalesce(IsClosed,is_closed,""))),"1","true"),1,0)
| eval open_opp=if(IsClosed_f=0,1,0)
| eval days_in_pipeline=if(isnotnull(cd_date), round((now()-cd_date)/86400,0), null())
| eval pipeline_amt=if(open_opp=1, Amount, 0)
| eval weighted_value=if(open_opp=1, Amount*Probability/100, null())
| eval won_amt=if(IsWon_f=1, Amount, 0)
| eval lost_amt=if(IsClosed_f=1 AND IsWon_f=0, Amount, 0)
| bin _time span=1d
| stats sum(pipeline_amt) as total_pipeline sum(weighted_value) as weighted_pipeline sum(won_amt) as won_revenue sum(lost_amt) as lost_revenue avg(eval(if(open_opp=1, days_in_pipeline, null()))) as avg_days_in_pipeline dc(Id) as opportunity_count sum(IsWon_f) as won_deals sum(eval(if(IsClosed_f=1 AND IsWon_f=0,1,0))) as lost_deals by ForecastCategoryName, _time
| eventstats sum(total_pipeline) as org_open_pipe sum(won_revenue) as org_won_revenue
| eval win_rate=if((won_deals+lost_deals)>0, round(100*won_deals/(won_deals+lost_deals),1), null())
| eval pipeline_coverage=if(org_won_revenue>0, round(org_open_pipe/nullif(org_won_revenue,0),2), null())
| eval coverage_flag=if(isnotnull(pipeline_coverage) AND pipeline_coverage<3, "below_3x", "ok")
| table _time, ForecastCategoryName, total_pipeline, weighted_pipeline, won_revenue, win_rate, avg_days_in_pipeline, pipeline_coverage, coverage_flag, opportunity_count
| sort _time, ForecastCategoryName
```

## Visualization

Use Dashboard Studio: `splunk.funnel` with opportunity counts by `StageName` in process order, then `Closed Won` and `Closed Lost`. Add `splunk.singlevalue` for weighted open pipeline with a quarter-over-quarter delta; trellised `splunk.gauge` of pipeline coverage by `ForecastCategoryName`; a line chart with current-quarter and previous-quarter `weighted_value`; and a `splunk.table` of `OwnerId` with `total_pipeline` and win rate for rep drilldown. Anchor footnotes to the same Salesforce reports used in Step 3 for reconciliation.

## Known False Positives

Quarter-end sandbagging is the classic CRM pattern where reps move **CloseDate** out of the current fiscal quarter in the last few business days to protect recognized quota, which temporarily shrinks `pipeline_amt` and makes **pipeline_coverage** look worse even though the quarter is still on track. A mid-quarter **OwnerId** mass-change from territory realignment (account-team or sharing-rule churn) reassigns hundreds of opps in one batch; for two to three days your per-rep and per-region cut of the same SPL can swing while territories settle. A deal that went **Closed Lost** and was later re-opened to **Negotiation/Review** then **Closed Won** looks like a brand-new long-cycle opp if you only read point-in-time `Opportunity` rows without **OpportunityFieldHistory**, so `avg_days_in_pipeline` and velocity can look inflated. Subscription-plus-services quotes that explode into many **OpportunityLineItem** children can also distort booking-like totals if you accidentally count line-level ingest rows against parent **Amount**. Forecasting users sometimes override the category that **StageName** would imply; the visible **ForecastCategoryName** on the **Opportunity** can lag what **ForecastingItem** shows during a forecast-commit window, so Splunk and the in-app forecast subtotal will disagree for a few days. A sandbox refresh that replays ETL or bulk-updates `LastModifiedDate` on every record looks like a sudden spike in “changed” opps, and a VP-sales pipeline-scrub session on Friday afternoons can spike stage-transition rates without a genuine demand drop.

## References

- [Splunk Add-on for Salesforce (Splunkbase 4146)](https://splunkbase.splunk.com/app/4146)
- [Splunk DB Connect (Splunkbase 2686)](https://splunkbase.splunk.com/app/2686)
- [Salesforce Bulk API 2.0 reference (asynchronous, query jobs, results as CSV)](https://developer.salesforce.com/docs/atlas.en-us.api_asynch.meta/api_asynch/asynch_api_reference.htm)
- [Create a Connected App (OAuth, JWT, digital signature)](https://help.salesforce.com/s/articleView?id=sf.connected_app_create.htm)
- [Splunk — Use the HTTP Event Collector](https://docs.splunk.com/Documentation/Splunk/latest/Data/UsetheHTTPEventCollector)
